title: Hearing - Sales and/or Use Tax (State,Local,MTA) - 202511016H
text:
SOAH DOCKET NO. 304-25-17608

CPA HEARING NO. 119,645

 


RE:      **************


TAXPAYER NO:  **************

AUDIT OFFICE:   **************

AUDIT PERIOD:   September 1, 2016 THROUGH March 31, 2020


Direct Payment/RFD

BEFORE THE COMPTROLLER OF PUBLIC ACCOUNTS

OF THE STATE OF TEXAS


KELLY HANCOCK
Acting Texas Comptroller of Public Accounts


KAREN MATLOCK

Representing Respondent

 

**************

Representing Claimant


 



COMPTROLLER’S DECISION
 

This decision is considered final on December 2, 2025, unless a motion for rehearing is timely filed; this date of finality is calculated based on the Administrative Procedure Act (APA).[1]  The failure to timely file a motion for rehearing may result in adverse legal consequences.


Administrative Law Judge (ALJ) Kimberly LaFlair of the State Office of Administrative Hearings (SOAH) issued a Proposal for Decision (PFD) that includes Findings of Fact and Conclusions of Law.  SOAH served the PFD on each party and each party was given an opportunity to file exceptions and replies with SOAH in accordance with SOAH’s rules of procedure.  The ALJ recommended that the Comptroller adopt the PFD as written.


After review and consideration, IT IS ORDERED that the PFD is adopted as changed.[2] 


The result from this Decision is Attachment A.  The ALJ’s recommendation letter is Attachment B.  The PFD as changed is Attachment C.  Attachments A, B, and C are incorporated by reference.


Attachment A reflects a zero amount due.

 

SIGNED on this 7th day of November 2025
 

KELLY HANCOCK
Acting Comptroller of Public Accounts


By: Lisa Craven

Deputy Comptroller


Attachment A, Texas Notification of Hearing Results
Attachment B, ALJ’s recommendation letter
Attachment C, Proposal for Decision as changed

 

 

 


ATTACHMENT B


 

 

State Office of Administrative Hearings



Kristofer S. Monson 

Chief Administrative Law Judge

 

October 29, 2025

 

Karen Matlock                                                             

Texas Comptroller of Public Accounts

 

**************                                                             

COMPANY A

 

RE: SOAH Docket Number 304-25-17608.27;

Texas Comptroller of Public Accounts Hearing No. 119,645

 

 

Dear Parties:

 

Please be advised that Petitioner filed exceptions to the Proposal for Decision (PFD) in this matter on October 27, 2025. Staff filed a response on October 28, 2025. The Administrative Law Judge (ALJ) reviewed the exceptions and the response and recommends that the exceptions be rejected.

 

Having reviewed the filings of the parties and finding no errors in the findings of fact or conclusions of law, I recommend that the PFD be adopted as written. Because SOAH has concluded its involvement in the matter, the case is being remanded to the Texas Comptroller of Public Accounts. See Tex. Gov’t Code § 2003.051(a).

 

Kimberly LaFlair,


Presiding Administrative Law Judge

 

 

 

ATTACHMENT C


 

 

SOAH Docket No. 304-25-17608                                                             

Before the State Office of Administrative Hearings

TCPA Hearing No. 119,645

 

 

Proposal for Decision

 

************** (Claimant) requested a refund hearing when the Tax Division (Staff ) of the Texas Comptroller of Public Accounts (Comptroller) partially denied its refund claim for sales and use taxes it contends it accrued in error. Claimant submitted additional documentation, and an amended refund audit report was issued, increasing the refund amount. Claimant contends it is due additional refunds. Staff disagrees and contends no additional refunds are warranted. In this Proposal for Decision, the Administrative Law Judge (ALJ) finds that the denial of the remainder of the refund claim should be upheld.

 

I. Notice, Jurisdiction, and Procedural History

 

Staff referred this case to the State Office of Administrative Hearings (SOAH) and, on May 6, 2025, ALJ Kimberly LaFlair issued an order scheduling the hearing on the merits for July 8, 2025, and setting pre-hearing requirements including the requirements for the Zoom videoconference hearing. On June 13, 2025, Petitioner filed an unopposed motion to convert the hearing to written submission, and an order granting the motion and setting the written submission hearing was issued on June 17, 2025. Karen D. Matlock represented Staff, and ************** of COMPANY A represented Claimant. The record closed on August 18, 2025.

 

There are no issues of notice or jurisdiction; therefore, those matters are set out in the Findings of Fact and Conclusions of Law without further discussion.

 

II. REASONS FOR DECISION

 

A. Evidence

 

Claimant submitted the following exhibits for the written submission hearing: [ENDNOTE 3]

 


	
	30 Day Documentation Request;
	
	
	Summary of Items at Issue;
	
	
	Invoice Documentation; and
	
	
	Product Research.
	


 

Staff submitted the pleadings exchanged between the parties prior to referring the matter to SOAH and offered the following exhibits:

 


	
	Hearing Request Receipt;
	
	
	Refund Audit Report;
	
	
	Refund Audit Plan;
	
	
	Amended Audit Report;
	
	
	Amended Audit Plan;
	
	
	Refund Claim; and
	
	
	Amended Contested Items Spreadsheet.
	


 

The parties’ exhibits were admitted to the record without objection.

 

B. Agreed Adjustments

 

Staff agreed to the additional refund amounts as shown in its Exhibit 7.

 

C. Material Facts and Issues Presented

 

During the period at issue, Claimant operated a hospital facility in CITY, Texas. Claimant requested a refund of sales and use tax for the period of September 1, 2016, through March 31, 2020, which was granted in part. Claimant requested a refund hearing for the denied portion of each claim and provided additional documentation. Staff issued an amended refund audit report increasing the refund. Claimant is seeking additional refunds, contending several of the items denied and assessed during the refund audit are either exempt from Texas sales and use tax or were nontaxable services.

 

For the written submission hearing, Claimant filed argument and offered evidence including a list of the items in contention, purchase invoices, and product research information. In its argument, Claimant identified the areas of contention that remain – intravenous (IV) systems and prosthetic devices and supplies. Staff contends no additional refunds are warranted and referred the matter to SOAH.

 

D. ALJ Analysis and Recommendation

 

Texas imposes a tax on each sale of a taxable item in this state. Tex. Tax Code § 151.051. The term “taxable item” includes tangible personal property and taxable services. Id. §§ 151.009, .010. If the Comptroller finds that an amount of tax, penalty, or interest has been unlawfully or erroneously collected, the Comptroller shall credit or refund the amount. Id. § 111.104(a). When a taxpayer requests a refund, it must establish by a preponderance of evidence that taxes were erroneously collected or paid. See 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 1.26(e); see also, e.g., Comptroller’s Decision No. 109,787 (2015). However, if a taxpayer relies on an exemption to establish the error, then it must provide clear and convincing evidence. See 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 1.26(c); Comptroller’s Decision No. 100,477 (2012).

 

Exemptions from taxation are subject to strict construction because they are the antithesis of equality and uniformity and because they place a greater burden on other taxpaying businesses and individuals. See Bullock v. National BancShares Corp., 584 S.W.2d 268 (Tex. 1979). An exemption cannot be raised by implication, but must affirmatively appear, and all doubts are resolved in favor of the taxing authority and against a claimant. See, e.g., Comptroller’s Decision Nos. 41,522 and 44,260 (2010). In a contested case hearing, a taxpayer must produce sufficient records and supporting documentation appropriate to the tax or fee for the transactions in question to substantiate and enable verification of the taxpayer’s claim related to the amount of tax, penalty, or interest to be assessed, collected, or refunded. 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 1.26(a).

 

1. IV Systems

 

Sales or use tax is not due on the sale of medical equipment meeting the definition of IV systems, supplies, and replacement parts when those items are used in the treatment of humans. See Tex. Tax Code § 151.313(a)(15); 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 3.284(d)(9). A product is an IV system if, regardless of whether the product is designed or intended to be inserted subcutaneously into any part of the body, the product is designed or intended to be used to administer fluids, electrolytes, blood and blood products, or drugs to patients or to withdraw blood or fluids from patients. See Tex. Tax Code § 151.313(e). IV systems and their components and supplies include access ports, adapters, bags and bottles, cannulae, cassettes, catheters, clamps, connectors, drip chambers, extension sets, filters, in-line ports, luer locks, needles, poles, pumps and batteries, spikes, tubing, valves, volumetric chambers, and items designed or intended to connect qualifying products to one another or secure qualifying products to a patient. See Tex. Tax Code § 151.313(e); 34 Tex. Tax Code § 3.284(d)(9).

 

Claimant contends that the catheters at issue in this hearing can be designed or intended to be used simultaneously to administer a certain drug and surgically remove a blood clot, and that such multi-function catheters remain exempt because the statute does not require exclusive use. In 2023, however, the Comptroller rejected this argument holding that, “applying the plain language of Texas Tax Code § 151.313(e), it is the design and intended use of an item to administer fluids, electrolytes, blood and blood products, or drugs to patients or to withdraw blood or fluids from patients that determines whether the item qualifies as an exempt IV system and not the characteristics, abilities, or actual functions incident or supplemental to the design or intended use of the item.” Comptroller’s Decision No. 117,370 (2023). Items that may be used or are used to administer fluids, electrolytes, blood and blood products, or drugs to patients or to withdraw blood or fluids from patients, but are not designed or intended for such use, are not exempt IV systems. Id. The decision also held that a catheter used to inject saline to irrigate the tip of an electrode and cool the ablation site during a procedure was not an exempt IV system. Id. Additionally, the decision held that although a catheter could deliver contrast material during angioplasty procedure, the design and intended use of the catheter was to facilitate the insertion and guidance of occlusion catheters, infusion catheters, or other appropriate microcatheters, and therefore did not qualify as an exempt IV system. Id.

 

Claimant argues that Comptroller’s Decision No. 117,370 (2023) improperly indicates a statutory requirement regarding the exclusive use of an item, which goes outside the bounds of both the applicable statute and administrative rule, and that the statutory language should be further construed by considering its legislative history, specifically citing legislative history surrounding the most   recent amendment to the statute in 2013.

 

The objective in statutory construction is to give effect to the Legislature’s intent, “which we ascertain from the plain meaning of the words used in the statute” because the best indicator of what the Legislature intended is what it enacted. SW Royalties, Inc. v. Hegar, 500 S.W.3d 400, 404 (Tex. 2016); see also Combs v. Roark Amusement & Vending, L.P., 422 S.W.3d 632, 635 (Tex. 2013). Therefore, “[w]here text is clear, text is determinative of that intent.” Entergy Gulf States, Inc. v. Summers, 282 S.W.3d 433, 437 (Tex. 2009). It is presumed lawmakers chose statutory language “with care and that every word or phrase was used with a purpose in mind.” Tex. Lottery Comm’n v. First State Bank of DeQueen, 325 S.W.3d 628, 635 (Tex. 2010). Words and phrases used in a statute are read in context and construed according to the rules of grammar and common usage. Tex. Gov’t Code § 311.011; see also TGS-NOPEC Geophysical Co. v. Combs, 340 S.W.3d 432, 439 (Tex. 2011) (“Undefined terms in a statute are typically given their ordinary meaning [unless] a different or more precise definition is apparent from the term’s use in the context of the statute.”). Words and phrases are not considered in isolation; rather, “we consider the statute as a whole, giving effect to each provision so that none is rendered meaningless or mere surplusage.” TIC Energy & Chem., Inc. v. Martin, 498 S.W.3d 68, 74 (Tex. 2016); see also Brazos Elec. Power Coop., Inc. v. Texas Comm’n on Envtl. Quality, 576 S.W.3d 374, 384 17-1003, 2019 WL 1966835, at *6–7 (Tex. May 3, 2019).

 

The ALJ concludes the plain language of the statute is unambiguous and conforms to the discussions set out in the cited legislative history, and that Claimant’s contention that an item qualifies as an exempt IV system or component “once it has been established that the items are functionally designed and capable of administering or withdrawing fluids” is incorrect considering the statutory language.

 

a) CATHETER A

 

Claimant argues that this item is “designed to facilitate electrophysiological mapping of the hearing and to transmit radiofrequency (RF) energy to the catheter tip electrode for ablation purposes.” It also states that, during ablation, the catheter injects saline into a patient via the tip of the electrode to irrigate and cool the ablation site and to transmit RF energy to the patient for ablation purposes. In support, Claimant provided evidence in the form of FDA-approved labeling showing the product’s design and/or intended use. The IFU Labeling, Claimant argues, shows that this catheter is indicated for use in delivering heparinized saline and RF currents (considered a drug) to the patient.

 

Staff contends this catheter is not exempt from tax due to similarities to items included in Comptroller’s Decision 117,370, and it argues that Claimant has merely shown that the item may be used to administer a cooling solution during the ablation process and has not shown that it is designed or intended to administer fluids, electrolytes, blood and blood products, or drugs to patients or to withdraw blood or fluids from patients. Based on the record evidence, this item is designed and intended to treat atrial flutter.

 

The ALJ finds that while there is some evidence to support the catheter may also be used to facilitate infusion, the evidence is insufficient to demonstrate that the design and intended use of this catheter qualifies it as an exempt IV system or component or supply of an exempt IV System. The refund claim for the CATHETER A should remain denied.

 

b) CATHETER B

 

Claimant argues that the IFU Labeling shows that this catheter is intended for use in delivering heparinized saline and RF currents to the patient, and that Staff’s assertion that the purpose of the solution is to cool and irrigate the site is applying the wrong standard in denying the exemption.

 

Staff contends that these catheters operate similar to the catheter discussed above, because they are designed and intended for the ablation process. Additionally, Staff argues that, although saline solution may be injected during the ablation, the solution is to “cool and irrigate the site.”

 

Considering the evidence regarding the design and intended use of this item, the ALJ concludes Claimant failed to demonstrate that it qualifies as exempt IV systems or components or parts related to exempt IV systems. The refund claimed for the purchases of the CATHETER B should remain denied.

 

c) CATHETER C

 

As with the above items, Claimant contends that this catheter’s intended use exempts it from taxation as it is for the introduction of “interventional and infusion of diagnostic therapeutic agents into the neuro peripheral and coronary vasculatures,” as stated in the product’s brochure. Staff argues, however, that Claimant’s documentation states that the catheter is “engineered to prevent ovalization and deliver devices with minimal friction. The ultimate delivery platform for coil and stent deployment.” Additionally, Staff argues that Claimant did not provide sufficient documentation or information to show that the items in this category qualify as an exempt IV system.

 

Though there is no dispute that the catheter can simultaneously administer a certain drug and surgically remove a blood clot infusion, the ALJ finds that the design and intended use of the CATHETER C does not qualify it as an exempt IV system or component or supply of an exempt IV System. The refund claim for this product should remain denied.

 

2. Prosthetic Devices and Supplies

 

Sales or use tax is not due on the sale of medical equipment meeting the definition of a prosthetic device or the replacement parts designed for a prosthetic device. See Tex. Tax Code § 151.313(a)(5); 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 3.284(d)(1). A “prosthetic device” is an item that is artificial and replaces a missing part of the body, performs the function of a vital organ or appendage of the human body, or is permanently implanted in the body. 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 3.284(a)(13). Examples of prosthetic devices are heart-lung pumps, nasal gastric and gastrointestinal devices, ureteral stents, urethral stents, artificial kidney machines, and related components and supplies. Id.

 

The Comptroller has held that non-prosthetic items that are simply used for delivery and not related to the use or function of the implanted prosthetic device are not exempt. Comptroller Decision No. 117,370 (2023). Also see STAR Document No. 201912014L (December 18, 2019), to determine when accessories, components, or supplies used along with an exempt prosthetic device are considered to qualify for exemption as “related” to the device. In that letter, the Comptroller’s Tax Policy Division found that a wireless remote and charger were exempt components of an implanted exempt prosthetic pain-management device. While the remote and charger were not prosthetic devices on their own, the Comptroller’s Tax Policy Division determined they were “necessary for the patient to use the implant to control pain and to keep the battery functioning.” Based on the necessity of the remote and charger for the function of the prosthetic device once implanted, the Comptroller’s Tax Policy Division determined the items were exempt as “related components and supplies” under Texas Tax Code § 151.313(a)(5) and 34 Texas Administrative Code § 3.284(a)(13). See STAR Document Nos. 201912014L; 200903254L (March 23, 2009), 9710965L (October 15, 1997; superseded January 23, 2023) (stents that remain implanted in the heart are exempt as prosthetic devices, but cardiac balloon catheters and cardiac guide wires used to place the stents are not exempt); Comptroller’s Decision Nos. 114,274 (2018) (partially superseded February 1, 2023 on unrelated grounds) (a device that merely facilitates placement of a exempt prosthetic device does not qualify for the exemption), 19,036 (1987) (a pacemaker implanted in the body is an exempt prosthetic device; the related “programmer,” though not implanted in the body, performed the functions of the brain, spinal cord, sympathetic and parasympathetic nervous systems, endocrine glands, and adrenal glands and was therefore also an exempt prosthetic device).”

 

a) Single-Use Supplies Sold on Same Invoice with Prosthetic Devices

 

    (i) DEVICE A

 

DEVICE A is described by Claimant as an implant inserted into the heart through a catheter placed into a vein, reducing the risk of stroke. On the transaction in question, the DEVICE A implantable device was priced separately on the same invoice from the DEVICE A delivery system and the DEVICE A. Claimant provided evidence in the form of the FDA- approved labeling that it contends supports that this access system is specifically designed to deliver DEVICE A implants and therefore constitutes an exempt component or supply to a prosthetic device.

 

An item that merely facilitates placement of an exempt prosthetic device does not qualify for the exemption. Comptroller’s Decision No. 114,274. Staff contends, and cites Comptroller’s Decision No. 117,370 as support, that the items for which Claimant seeks a refund are “not related to the use or function of the implanted prosthetic device” because they are “simply used for delivery.” Claimant disputes the precedent (i.e., Comptroller’s Decision No. 117,370) requiring non-prosthetic items such as the catheters be related to the use or function of the implanted prosthetic device to qualify for the exemption, arguing such a standard is not found in the language of the statute of rule and the application of such a standard such “exceeds the agency’s authority.” Considering the evidence in the record and the cited precedent and policy, the ALJ finds that these items include delivery and placement mechanisms for use in implanting prosthetic devices and are therefore not subject to the exemption.

 

b) Single-Use Supplies Sold on Separate Invoice from Prosthetic Device

 

The discussion of single-use supplies sold on a separate invoice from the prosthetic device includes the following:

 

CATHETER D

CATHETER E

CATHETER G

CATHETER H

CATHETER J

 

Claimant provided evidence in the form of the IFU labeling, which it contends supports that these catheters can deploy implanted stents. Staff argues that these items are merely delivery systems, which are not exempt. As in the section above, the ALJ finds the items set out above include delivery and placement mechanisms for use in implanting prosthetic devices, which are not exempt from sales and use tax.

 

c) Non-Preloaded Prosthetic Supplies

 

The discussion of non-preloaded prosthetic supplies sold on a separate invoice from the prosthetic device includes the following:

 

STAPLER

DEVICE B

DEVICE C

 

According to information provided by the Claimant, the STAPLER delivers permanently implanted staples, the DEVICE B is for use in endoscopic surgery for the placement of interrupted or running stitches in soft tissues, and the DEVICE C is an anchor system for a surgical anastomosis (a surgical procedure where two tubular structures, such as blood vessels, intestines, or urinary tracts, are joined together). The anchor device is implanted by the DEVICE C. Claimant contends that its evidence, the IFU and product brochure labeling, supports its position that these supplies can deploy implanted stents.

 

Staff argues that the supplies are delivery systems and therefore not exempt. The ALJ agrees and recommends this contention be denied.

 

d) Preloaded Prosthetic Devices

 

The items that remain at issue related to this contention are types of surgical instruments that are preloaded by the manufacturer, deploy prosthetic devices (staples, tacks, clips, etc.), and are sold for a single price. Claimant contends that the preloaded devices consist of an implant contained and manufactured within a delivery component, comprising one indivisible device, and that the implant cannot be deployed without its deployment component. Claimant argues that related components or supplies of prosthetic devices qualify for exemption to the same extent as the prosthetic devices, themselves. Additionally, Claimant argues that since the items at issue constitute prosthetic devices, and the applier is a component part of the item, it constitutes a “related component and supply” to a prosthetic device.

 

In a recent decision, the Comptroller considered this same device and these same arguments. The Comptroller concluded that the item is a delivery and placement mechanism for use in implanting a prosthetic device and held that the item did not qualify as an exempt prosthetic device. Comptroller’s Decision No. 117,370 (2023). As discussed above, a delivery device does not qualify as an exempt prosthetic device, and the essence of the transaction analysis does not apply. See Comptroller’s Decision No. 117,370 (2023).

 

Considering the evidence in the record and the cited precedent and policy, the ALJ finds the items at issue in this contention include delivery and placement mechanisms for use in implanting prosthetic devices. Claimant is correct in stating the delivery device works with the prosthetic device in implanting the prosthetic. However, that symbiotic relationship does not establish the delivery device as a related component part or supply for the prosthetic. The cited items do not qualify as exempt prosthetic devices, and the cost of any incorporated prosthetic devices included along with the non-prosthetic delivery system or mechanism has not been demonstrated. See Comptroller’s Decision No. 117,370; STAR Document No. 201004232L (April 29, 2010) (applying the predominant cost test to the sale of a preloaded, single-use stapler, acknowledging the staples themselves were exempt but finding “[t]he skin stapler which holds the staples is considered a device or tool used by the health care provider”). The refund claim for these devices should remain denied.

 

e) Neuromodulation

 

Claimant contends that the items below are programmers and supplies that are exempt prosthetic devices and supplies because they control an implanted device that controls a function of the brain.

 

PROGRAMMER DEVICE

DEVICE D

DEVICE E

 

Staff asserts that, according to the product information submitted by Claimant, these products are for nerve stimulation and do not qualify for the exemption. Although these devices and parts are for treating some neurological disorders, such as tremors through nerve stimulation, Staff argues they do not perform a vital function of the brain. See Comptroller’s Decision No. 117,370 (2023) (holding that an implanted heart monitor system did not perform a function of a vital organ).

 

Considering the evidence in the record and the cited precedent and policy, the ALJ finds that Claimant failed to establish that the items in this category are exempt prosthetic devices and this contention should remain denied.

 

f) Implanted Prosthetic Devices

 

Claimant contends that the following items qualify for exemption because they are implanted:

 

************** (insertable cardiac monitor) 

************** (insertable cardiac monitor)

 

Claimant states that these items are implantable monitoring systems that record subcutaneous electrocardiogram, which is a test that records the timing and strength of the electrical signals that make the heartbeat. Additionally, Claimant states that these devices are implanted subcutaneously to capture electrocardiogram recordings for patients with cardiac arrythmias. Claimant cites to various Comptroller opinions it contends support the Comptroller’s long-standing policy reflecting that the “permanence” of an implanted prosthetic device relates to the relatively long duration of implantation and/or the relative ease or difficulty of its removal.

 

Staff argues that these items do not perform a vital function of a vital organ or appendage of the human body nor are they implanted permanently into the body and again cites Comptroller’s Decision No. 117,370 (2023). Considering the evidence in the record and the cited precedent and policy, the ALJ finds that Claimant failed to establish that the items in this category are exempt prosthetic devices and this contention should remain denied.

 

III. Findings of Fact


	
	************** (Claimant) operated a hospital facility in CITY, Texas.
	
	
	Claimant requested a refund of sales and use tax for the period of September 1, 2016, through March 31, 2020, which was granted in part.
	
	
	Claimant requested a refund hearing for the denied portion of each claim and provided additional documentation.
	
	
	The Tax Division (Staff ) of the Texas Comptroller of Public Accounts (Comptroller) issued an amended refund audit report, increasing the refund.
	
	
	Staff did not agree to additional refund amounts and referred the matter to the State Office of Administrative Hearings (SOAH).
	
	
	On May 6, 2025, Administrative Law Judge (ALJ) Kimberly LaFlair issued an Order Scheduling Hearing on the Merits, and setting pre-hearing requirements, including the requirements for the videoconference hearing.
	
	
	On May 7, 2025, Staff issued Petitioner a Notice of Hearing. The notice contained a statement of the date, time, and location of the hearing, the nature of the hearing; a statement of the legal authority and jurisdiction under which the hearing was to be held; a reference to the particular sections of the statutes and rules involved; and a short, plain statement of the factual matters asserted, or an attachment that incorporated by reference the factual matters asserted in the complaints or petitions filed with the state agency.
	
	
	On June 13, 2025, Petitioner filed an unopposed motion to convert the hearing to written submission, and an order granting the motion and setting the written submission hearing was issued on June 17, 2025.
	
	
	Claimant filed argument and offered evidence including a list of the items in contention, purchase invoices, and product research information for the written submission hearing.
	
	
	The contested case record closed on August 18, 2025.
	


 

IV. Conclusions of Law


	
	The Comptroller has jurisdiction over this matter. See Tex. Tax Code ch. 111.
	
	
	SOAH has jurisdiction over matters related to the hearing in this matter, including the authority to issue a proposal for decision with findings of fact and conclusions of law. See Tex. Gov’t Code ch. 2003.
	
	
	Staff provided proper and timely notice of the hearing. See Tex. Gov’t Code ch. 2001; Tex. Tax Code § 111.105.
	
	
	Texas imposes a tax on each sale of a taxable item in this state. Tex. Tax Code § 151.051.
	
	
	The term “taxable item” includes tangible personal property and taxable services. Tex. Tax Code §§ 151.009, .010.
	
	
	If the Comptroller finds that an amount of tax, penalty, or interest has been unlawfully or erroneously collected, the Comptroller shall credit or refund the amount. Tex. Tax Code § 111.104(a).
	
	
	When a taxpayer requests a refund, it must establish by a preponderance of evidence that taxes were erroneously collected or paid. See 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 1.26(e); see also, e.g., Comptroller’s Decision No. 109,787 (2015).
	
	
	If a taxpayer relies on an exemption to establish the error, then it must provide clear and convincing evidence. See 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 1.26(c); Comptroller’s Decision No. 100,477 (2012).
	
	
	Exemptions from taxation are subject to strict construction because they are the antithesis of equality and uniformity and because they place a greater burden on other taxpaying businesses and individuals. See Bullock v. National BancShares Corp., 584 S.W.2d 268 (Tex. 1979).
	
	
	An exemption cannot be raised by implication, but must affirmatively appear, and all doubts are resolved in favor of the taxing authority and against a claimant. See, e.g., Comptroller’s Decision Nos. 41,522 and 44,260 (2010).
	
	
	In a contested case hearing, a taxpayer must produce sufficient records and supporting documentation appropriate to the tax or fee for the transactions in question to substantiate and enable verification of the taxpayer’s claim related to the amount of tax, penalty, or interest to be assessed, collected, or refunded. 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 1.26(a).
	
	
	Sales or use tax is not due on the sale of medical equipment meeting the definition of intravenous (IV) systems, supplies, and replacement parts when those items are used in the treatment of humans. See Tex. Tax Code § 151.313(a)(15); 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 3.284(d)(9).
	



	
	A product is an IV system if, regardless of whether the product is designed or intended to be inserted subcutaneously into any part of the body, the product is designed or intended to be used to administer fluids, electrolytes, blood and blood products, or drugs to patients or to withdraw blood or fluids from patients. See Tex. Tax Code § 151.313(e).
	
	
	IV systems and their components and supplies include access ports, adapters, bags and bottles, cannulae, cassettes, catheters, clamps, connectors, drip chambers, extension sets, filters, in-line ports, luer locks, needles, poles, pumps and batteries, spikes, tubing, valves, volumetric chambers, and items designed or intended to connect qualifying products to one another or secure qualifying products to a patient. See Tex. Tax Code § 151.313(e); 34 Tex. Tax Code § 3.284(d)(9).
	



	
	It is the design and intended use of an item to administer fluids, electrolytes, blood and blood products, or drugs to patients or to withdraw blood or fluids from patients that determines whether the item qualifies as an exempt IV system and not the characteristics, abilities, or actual functions incident or supplemental to the design or intended use of the item. Comptroller’s Decision No. 117,370 (2023).
	



	
	Items that may be used or are used to administer fluids, electrolytes, blood and blood products, or drugs to patients or to withdraw blood or fluids from patients, but are not designed or intended for such use, are not exempt IV systems. Comptroller’s Decision No. 117,370 (2023).
	



	
	A catheter used to inject saline to irrigate the tip of an electrode and cool the ablation site during a procedure is not an exempt IV system. Comptroller’s Decision No. 117,370 (2023).
	



	
	Although a catheter could deliver contrast material during angioplasty procedure, if the design and intended use of the catheter is to facilitate the insertion and guidance of occlusion catheters, infusion catheters, or other appropriate microcatheters, it does not qualify as an exempt IV system. Comptroller’s Decision No. 117,370 (2023).
	



	
	The objective in statutory construction is to give effect to the Legislature’s intent, “which we ascertain from the plain meaning of the words used in the statute” because the best indicator of what the Legislature intended is what it enacted. SW Royalties, Inc. v. Hegar, 500 S.W.3d 400, 404 (Tex. 2016); see also Combs v. Roark Amusement & Vending, L.P., 422 S.W.3d 632, 635 (Tex. 2013).
	
	
	Where text is clear, text is determinative of that intent. Entergy Gulf States, Inc. v. Summers, 282 S.W.3d 433, 437 (Tex. 2009).
	



	
	It is presumed lawmakers chose statutory language “with care and that every word or phrase was used with a purpose in mind.” Tex. Lottery Comm’n v. First State Bank of DeQueen, 325 S.W.3d 628, 635 (Tex. 2010).
	



	
	Words and phrases used in a statute are read in context and construed according to the rules of grammar and common usage. Tex. Gov’t Code § 311.011; see also TGS- NOPEC Geophysical Co. v. Combs, 340 S.W.3d 432, 439 (Tex. 2011) (Undefined terms in a statute are typically given their ordinary meaning [unless] a different or more precise definition is apparent from the term’s use in the context of the statute).
	



	
	Words and phrases are not considered in isolation; rather, “we consider the statute as a whole, giving effect to each provision so that none is rendered meaningless or mere surplusage.” TIC Energy & Chem., Inc. v. Martin, 498 S.W.3d 68, 74 (Tex. 2016); see also Brazos Elec. Power Coop., Inc. v. Texas Comm’n on Envtl. Quality, 576 S.W.3d 374, 384 17-1003, 2019 WL 1966835, at *6–7 (Tex. May 3, 2019).
	



	
	Applying the plain language of Texas Tax Code § 151.313(e), it is the design and intended use of an item to administer fluids, electrolytes, blood and blood products, or drugs to patients or to withdraw blood or fluids from patients that determines whether the item qualifies as an exempt IV system and not the characteristics, abilities, or actual functions incident or supplemental to the design or intended use of the item. Items that may be used or are used to administer fluids, electrolytes, blood and blood products, or drugs to patients or to withdraw blood or fluids from patients, but are not designed or intended for such use, are not exempt IV systems. See, e.g., Comptroller’s Decision No. 117,370 (2023).
	



	
	The ALJ finds that while there is some evidence to support the catheter may also be used to facilitate infusion; however, the evidence is insufficient to demonstrate that the design and intended use of the catheters at issue qualify them as an exempt IV system or component or supply of an exempt IV System.
	



	
	The refund claim for the catheters IV items should remain denied.
	


Source: See PFD, pp. 5-7; Conclusion of Law No. 25.


	
	Sales or use tax is not due on the sale of medical equipment meeting the definition of a prosthetic device or the replacement parts designed for a prosthetic device. See Tex. Tax Code § 151.313(a)(5); 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 3.284(d)(1).
	



	
	A “prosthetic device” is an item that is artificial and replaces a missing part of the body, performs the function of a vital organ or appendage of the human body, or is permanently implanted in the body. Examples of prosthetic devices are heart-lung pumps, nasal gastric and gastrointestinal devices, ureteral stents, urethral stents, artificial kidney machines, and related components and supplies. 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 3.284(a)(13).
	



	
	Non-prosthetic items that are simply used for delivery and not related to the use or function of the implanted prosthetic device are not exempt. Comptroller Decision No. 117,370 (2023), see also STAR Document No. 201912014L (December 18, 2019).
	



	
	Based on the necessity of an item for the function of a prosthetic device once implanted, the items are exempt as “related components and supplies” under Texas Tax Code § 151.313(a)(5) and 34 Texas Administrative Code § 3.284(a)(13). See STAR Document Nos. 201912014L; 200903254L (March 23, 2009), 9710965L (October 15, 1997; superseded January 23, 2023) (stents that remain implanted in the heart are exempt as prosthetic devices, but cardiac balloon catheters and cardiac guide wires used to place the stents are not exempt); Comptroller’s Decision Nos. 114,274 (2018) (partially superseded February 1, 2023 on unrelated grounds) (a device that merely facilitates placement of a exempt prosthetic device does not qualify for the exemption), 19,036 (1987) (a pacemaker implanted in the body is an exempt prosthetic device; the related “programmer,” though not implanted in the body, performed the functions of the brain, spinal cord, sympathetic and parasympathetic nervous systems, endocrine glands, and adrenal glands and was therefore also an exempt prosthetic device).”
	
	
	The prosthetic supplies at issue include delivery and placement mechanisms for use in implanting prosthetic devices and are therefore not exempt from sales and use tax.
	



	
	The ALJ finds that the items in contention do not qualify as exempt prosthetic devices, and the cost of any incorporated prosthetic devices included along with the non-prosthetic delivery system or mechanism has not been demonstrated. See Comptroller’s Decision No. 117,370; STAR Document No. 201004232L (April 29, 2010) (applying the predominant cost test to the sale of a preloaded, single-use stapler, acknowledging the staples themselves were exempt but finding “[t]he skin stapler which holds the staples is considered a device or tool used by the health care provider.”).
	



	
	The refund claim for these devices should remain denied.
	



	
	An implanted heart monitor system does not perform a function of a vital organ. Comptroller’s Decision No. 117,370 (2023).
	



	
	Claimant failed to establish that neuromodulation items and implanted prosthetic devices at issue are exempt prosthetic devices and supplies, and this contention should remain denied.
	


 

Signed October 10, 2025

 

Kimberly LaFlair

Presiding Administrative Law Judge

[2] See Tex. Gov’t Code § 2003.101(e) and (f).

subjects: AN ITEM’S "DESIGN OR INTENDED USE" DETERMINES WHETHER AN ITEM QUALIFIES FOR MEDICAL EXEMPTION, NOT THE ITEM’S FUNCTIONALITY OR CAPABILITY OF HOW IT "COULD" BE USED
